article_title: I built a full productivity stack without paying a dime
core_thesis: The author demonstrates that a comprehensive, self-hosted productivity stack can be built entirely using free and open-source software (FOSS) on modest hardware, replacing cloud-based services that often degrade into ad-ridden, paywalled experiences. The stack covers file sharing, office documents, note-taking, password management, finance tracking, document management, fitness, and meal planning, all running locally on a home server with minimal upfront cost if existing hardware is repurposed.
detailed_summary: The article details the author's self-hosted productivity stack built entirely from free and open-source software, deployed on a home server (i3-N305 system, with an old Pascal GPU for LLMs). The core of the stack is Nextcloud, which provides file sharing, collaborative office editing via Collabora Online/Nextcloud Office, forms, calendar, RSS, recipes, and video calls. For note-taking, Blinko replaces multiple tools (Obsidian, Google Keep, Trilium Notes, Vikunja) with a minimalist interface, Markdown support, and LLM-powered features like RAG search, automatic summaries, and tag generation. Vaultwarden serves as a self-hosted password manager syncing with Bitwarden clients, handling passwords, TOTP, passkeys, bank cards, and identity documents. Firefly III manages finances with budgeting, recurring transactions, multi-currency support, and data import. Paperless-ngx (with Paperless-GPT and Paperless AI) handles document management with LLM-powered OCR, tagging, and querying. Wger and Mealie cover fitness tracking (workout plans, nutrition) and meal planning (recipe import, scaling, shopping lists). The author emphasizes that all services run as lightweight containers (LXCs) except Nextcloud (VM) and LLMs (Ollama LXC), and can run on ARM systems like Raspberry Pi. The only costs are electricity and existing hardware; no new purchases were needed.

entities.name: Ayush Pande
entities.role: Author of the article and builder of the productivity stack

entities.name: Nextcloud
entities.role: Self-hosted file sharing and productivity platform with office apps

entities.name: Blinko
entities.role: Self-hosted note-taking app with LLM features

entities.name: Vaultwarden
entities.role: Self-hosted password manager compatible with Bitwarden

entities.name: Wger
entities.role: Self-hosted fitness and workout tracking app

entities.name: Mealie
entities.role: Self-hosted recipe and meal planning app

entities.name: Paperless AI
entities.role: LLM-powered addon for Paperless-ngx for tagging and queries

entities.name: Ollama
entities.role: Local LLM runtime used for Blinko and Paperless AI/GPT

key_data_points: Nextcloud can be extended with Collabora Online and Nextcloud Office apps to create and edit documents, spreadsheets, and presentations directly from the web UI, rivaling Office 365 and Google Drive (🟢 relevant to your homelab: provides a full office suite without subscriptions).

Blinko replaces multiple note-taking and to-do apps (Obsidian, Google Keep, Trilium Notes, Vikunja) with a minimalist interface, Markdown support, and LLM-powered features like RAG search, automatic summary generation, and tag creation (🟢 relevant to your productivity: consolidates tools and leverages local LLMs for knowledge management).

Vaultwarden syncs with Bitwarden client apps and browser extensions, storing passwords, TOTP codes, API tokens, passkeys, bank cards, and identity documents locally (🟢 relevant to your cybersecurity: self-hosted password manager reduces reliance on cloud services and gives full control over secrets).

Firefly III supports multiple currencies (USD, EUR, INR) and flexible budgeting (not zero-sum), with a data importer for banking records (🟢 relevant to your student finances: helps track budgets and transactions without manual conversion).

Paperless-ngx with Paperless-GPT and Paperless AI provides LLM-powered OCR, automatic tagging, and querying on documents, and can import files from emails via automated rules (🟢 relevant to your homelab: demonstrates integration of local LLMs with document management).

The entire stack runs as lightweight containers (LXCs) on an i3-N305 system, with Nextcloud as a VM and LLMs in an Ollama LXC using an old Pascal GPU; it can also run on ARM systems like Raspberry Pi (🟢 relevant to your homelab: shows low-resource deployment and repurposing old hardware).

The author did not spend any extra money on hardware; the server uses a spare i3-N305 system and an old Pascal GPU, with minimal impact on energy bills after power optimization (🟢 relevant to your ambition: proves a full productivity stack is achievable with existing hardware).
methodology_steps: Deploy Nextcloud (preferably NextcloudPi) on a Raspberry Pi or as a VM on a home server. Install Collabora Online and Nextcloud Office apps from the Nextcloud app store to enable document editing.

Set up Blinko as a container (LXC or Docker) for note-taking. Configure LLM integration (e.g., via Ollama) for RAG search, automatic summaries, and tag generation. Use the random walk feature to revisit ideas.
Deploy Vaultwarden as a container. Configure it to sync with Bitwarden client apps and browser extensions. Import existing passwords and enable TOTP, passkeys, and identity storage.
Install Firefly III as a container. Set up the data importer to automatically pull banking records. Configure budgets, recurring transactions, and multi-currency accounts.
Deploy Paperless-ngx as a container. Add Paperless-GPT and Paperless AI containers for LLM-powered OCR, tagging, and querying. Configure email import rules to automatically ingest documents.
Set up Wger as a container for workout and nutrition tracking. Create exercise templates and meal plans. Optionally integrate with Mealie for recipe management.
Deploy Mealie as a container for recipe management. Use its import feature to pull recipes from websites. Create shopping lists and scale recipes to meet nutritional needs.

Run all services as LXCs on a Proxmox host (or similar) to minimize resource usage. Use an Ollama LXC for LLM inference, leveraging an old GPU if available. Optimize power settings to reduce energy consumption.
conclusions: A fully functional, self-hosted productivity stack can be built entirely with free and open-source software, eliminating subscription costs and privacy concerns associated with cloud services. The author successfully replaced Office 365, Google Drive, Obsidian, Google Keep, Bitwarden, and other paid/freemium tools with Nextcloud, Blinko, Vaultwarden, Firefly III, Paperless-ngx, Wger, and Mealie.

The stack is resource-efficient, running on modest hardware (i3-N305, N100, or Raspberry Pi) using containers, with only Nextcloud requiring a VM and LLMs needing a GPU. This makes it accessible to hobbyists and students with existing hardware.

Local LLMs enhance productivity tools significantly, enabling features like RAG search, automatic summarization, and intelligent tagging without relying on external APIs. This is a key differentiator for Blinko and Paperless-ngx.

The article underscores the importance of self-hosting for privacy and control, especially for sensitive data like passwords, financial records, and personal documents. The 3-2-1 backup strategy mentioned for these services is a best practice for data resilience.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in homelabbing, self-hosting, and productivity. It provides a concrete, actionable blueprint for building a complete FOSS productivity stack on low-cost hardware, which aligns with your ambition to take initiative and repurpose old equipment. The inclusion of Vaultwarden and Paperless-ngx touches on cybersecurity aspects (password management, document security), while Blinko's LLM integration and Firefly III's financial tracking are directly useful for a student managing notes and budgets. The article also offers technical details on containerization and LLM deployment that you can apply to your own homelab projects.
extra_advice: Consider extending this stack by integrating a self-hosted Git service (e.g., Gitea) for version control of your notes and code, and a monitoring tool (e.g., Uptime Kuma) to keep track of service health. Since you're into Red Teaming, you could also deploy a self-hosted vulnerability scanner (e.g., OpenVAS) or a SIEM (e.g., Wazuh) on the same hardware to practice offensive and defensive security in a controlled environment.
